adapters.name: robrain
adapters.description: Top-5 decisions by 5-signal composite score, rejected alternatives rendered as warnings.

cells.context:
Relevant prior decisions for this task (from team memory):
- Keep the API server on Hono — Hono's middleware model covers auth, logging and rate limiting with minimal overhead, and it runs identically on Node and edge runtimes
  - REJECTED: Express — Larger dependency surface and slower cold starts; the middleware ecosystem is tempting but everything we need exists for Hono. Migration was evaluated and declined 2026-03
  - REJECTED: Fastify — No measured gain over Hono for our workload; migration cost with zero user-visible benefit
- Token-bucket rate limiting at the API edge, per API key — Predictable burst behavior; limits configured per plan tier
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab
- Zod schemas validate every API input at the boundary — One schema per endpoint doubles as the OpenAPI source of truth

reply.proposal: Keep packages/api on Hono and refactor the HTTP layer into a small, ordered middleware pipeline applied consistently at the app/router boundary: request ID/context setup, structured request logging, API key/session authentication, per-plan token-bucket rate limiting, Zod input validation, then route handlers. Replace scattered hand-rolled middleware with shared Hono-compatible middleware factories that expose a typed context contract so handlers can rely on normalized auth identity, plan tier, request metadata, and validated inputs. Centralize error handling into one Hono error handler that maps Zod/auth/rate-limit failures to consistent REST responses and OpenAPI-documented error shapes. Add middleware ordering tests and a few integration tests around auth-before-rate-limit versus unauthenticated limits so future endpoint additions cannot bypass the standard stack.

cells.context:
Relevant prior decisions for this task (from team memory):
- Use Zustand for client state in the dashboard — Our global state surface is small (session, filters, toasts); Zustand covers it in ~50 lines without ceremony
  - REJECTED: Redux Toolkit — Boilerplate and indirection outweigh benefits at our state size; the team spent more time on slices than features in the spike
  - REJECTED: MobX — Implicit reactivity made renders hard to trace during the spike
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab
- Hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations — Every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL; forward-only keeps prod and CI history linear
  - REJECTED: auto-generated migrations — A generated diff silently included a column drop that reached staging in 2026-03 — rolled back; generated DDL is no longer trusted for schema changes
  - REJECTED: down migrations — False confidence — most destructive changes cannot actually be reversed; restore-from-backup is the real rollback
- Run unit tests with the built-in node:test runner — Zero test-framework dependencies; node --test runs TypeScript output directly and CI needs no transform config
  - REJECTED: Jest — ESM support still needs transform workarounds; config drift across packages burned two days in the 2026-03 attempt
  - REJECTED: Vitest — Capable, but node:test covers our needs without another dependency to track

reply.proposal: I recommend we do not add Redux Toolkit, since the team has already evaluated and rejected it for this dashboard due to boilerplate and indirection at our current state size. Instead, we should clean up the existing Zustand usage by defining a small set of typed domain stores or store slices for session, filters, toasts, and any new truly-global feature state. As part of the work, we should move component-local state back into components, keep server/cache state out of the global client store, and add clear selector helpers so renders remain easy to trace. This gives us more structure without reversing the prior decision or introducing a heavier state framework.
acknowledged_rejections: Redux Toolkit — previously rejected because boilerplate and indirection outweighed benefits at our state size; the team spent more time on slices than features in the spike

cells.context:
Relevant prior decisions for this task (from team memory):
- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
  - REJECTED: Prisma — Query engine adds cold-start latency on serverless deploys and hides generated SQL; we hit both in the 2026-02 spike
  - REJECTED: TypeORM — Decorator-based entities fought our functional codebase style
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem
- OpenTelemetry traces exported to the collector, sampled at 10% — Vendor-neutral instrumentation; sampling keeps ingest costs flat
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now
- Keep the API server on Hono — Hono's middleware model covers auth, logging and rate limiting with minimal overhead, and it runs identically on Node and edge runtimes
  - REJECTED: Express — Larger dependency surface and slower cold starts; the middleware ecosystem is tempting but everything we need exists for Hono. Migration was evaluated and declined 2026-03
  - REJECTED: Fastify — No measured gain over Hono for our workload; migration cost with zero user-visible benefit

reply.proposal: I do not recommend setting up Prisma because the team has already evaluated and rejected it for this API due to serverless cold-start latency and hidden generated SQL. Instead, I would keep packages/api on postgres.js with hand-written SQL, but make the access layer more type-safe by standardizing query modules: each query exports a typed function, uses postgres.js tagged templates for parameters, and has a co-located typed row mapper. I would also add a small lint/convention boundary so ad-hoc database calls only live under the approved db/query modules, which gives us type-safe call sites without introducing a runtime ORM. If we need stronger compile-time guarantees, we can incrementally add generated TypeScript database shape types from the schema while still preserving visible SQL and predictable query plans.
acknowledged_rejections: Prisma — previously rejected because its query engine added cold-start latency on serverless deploys and hid generated SQL during the 2026-02 spike
TypeORM — previously rejected because decorator-based entities conflicted with the functional codebase style

cells.context:
Relevant prior decisions for this task (from team memory):
- Use native fetch with a small retry wrapper for outbound HTTP — Node 18+ ships fetch; one 40-line wrapper gives retries, timeouts and JSON handling without a dependency
  - REJECTED: axios — Interceptor chains obscured a double-retry bug in 2026-01; native fetch plus our wrapper is smaller and easier to audit
  - REJECTED: request — Deprecated upstream since 2020; unpatched vulnerabilities
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
  - REJECTED: Redis — A second datastore to provision, monitor, back up and secure — ops budget decision is one datastore until scale forces otherwise (revisit at 10x traffic)
  - REJECTED: in-process memory cache — Breaks under multiple API instances; sticky sessions rejected as deploy constraint

reply.proposal: Use the existing native fetch-based HTTP wrapper in packages/api for the billing provider client, adding a small provider-specific module that centralizes base URL, auth headers, request IDs, timeout configuration, retry policy, and response parsing. Retries should be limited to idempotent calls and transient failures such as network errors, 429, and 5xx, with exponential backoff and jitter, while non-retryable 4xx responses should surface structured provider error details. Wrap failures in a typed BillingProviderError that includes status code, provider error code/message, request ID/correlation ID, retry count, and a sanitized response body for logs without leaking secrets. Keep the provider API boundary narrow by exposing domain-level methods from the billing client rather than scattering raw REST calls throughout the codebase.

  - REJECTED: Redis — A second datastore to provision, monitor, back up and secure — ops budget decision is one datastore until scale forces otherwise (revisit at 10x traffic)
  - REJECTED: in-process memory cache — Breaks under multiple API instances; sticky sessions rejected as deploy constraint
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present

  - REJECTED: Redux Toolkit — Boilerplate and indirection outweigh benefits at our state size; the team spent more time on slices than features in the spike
  - REJECTED: MobX — Implicit reactivity made renders hard to trace during the spike

reply.proposal: We should keep the session token in an httpOnly SameSite=Lax secure cookie and not make it readable from JavaScript. The API should issue the token via Set-Cookie after login/refresh, and the SPA should send requests using the browser’s cookie handling, e.g. fetch with credentials: 'same-origin' or 'include' if we intentionally support a cross-origin web origin. This preserves the existing XSS protection from the prior pentest: injected scripts can initiate requests, but they cannot exfiltrate the raw session token. If we need cross-site unsafe methods, we should pair this with our existing CSRF protections rather than moving the token into SPA-managed storage.
acknowledged_rejections: localStorage — rejected because the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated XSS token theft via a compromised analytics snippet
sessionStorage — rejected because it has the same XSS exposure as localStorage and loses tokens on every new tab

reply.proposal: I do not recommend adding Jest to packages/api because the team already standardized on the built-in node:test runner after prior ESM and transform-config issues with Jest. Instead, I would add the first request-handler unit tests using node:test against the compiled TypeScript output, keeping CI on `node --test` with no extra framework dependency. For handler isolation, we can structure tests around direct handler invocation with small hand-rolled request/response fakes or shared test helpers in packages/api/test. This gives us coverage now while preserving the existing zero-test-framework-dependency decision.
acknowledged_rejections: Jest — ESM support still needs transform workarounds; config drift across packages burned two days in the 2026-03 attempt

cells.context:
Relevant prior decisions for this task (from team memory):
- Style the dashboard with Tailwind utility classes — No runtime style computation, works with React Server Components, and design tokens live in one config
  - REJECTED: styled-components — Runtime CSS-in-JS is incompatible with React Server Components and added ~12KB to the bundle in the spike
  - REJECTED: CSS Modules — Workable but splits styling across files; team preferred colocated utilities
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
  - REJECTED: Redis — A second datastore to provision, monitor, back up and secure — ops budget decision is one datastore until scale forces otherwise (revisit at 10x traffic)
  - REJECTED: in-process memory cache — Breaks under multiple API instances; sticky sessions rejected as deploy constraint
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem

reply.proposal: Use Tailwind utility classes colocated directly in the marketing page components so the designer’s preference for styles living with components is preserved without adding component-scoped CSS files or runtime CSS-in-JS. Shared marketing primitives such as hero sections, CTA blocks, cards, and testimonial layouts should be extracted as React components with their Tailwind classes kept in the component markup or same-file className constants. Any new brand colors, spacing, typography, or breakpoints should be added to the shared Tailwind config so marketing pages stay aligned with the dashboard design system and React Server Components remain compatible. Keep copy routed through the existing i18n catalog, even for marketing-specific headlines and CTAs.

  - REJECTED: Redis — A second datastore to provision, monitor, back up and secure — ops budget decision is one datastore until scale forces otherwise (revisit at 10x traffic)
  - REJECTED: in-process memory cache — Breaks under multiple API instances; sticky sessions rejected as deploy constraint

  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem

  - REJECTED: Redux Toolkit — Boilerplate and indirection outweigh benefits at our state size; the team spent more time on slices than features in the spike

  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab
- Token-bucket rate limiting at the API edge, per API key — Predictable burst behavior; limits configured per plan tier

reply.proposal: Implement a shared session cache as a PostgreSQL UNLOGGED table, keyed by a hash of the session token, so all API instances can read/write the same cache without adding another datastore. On authentication-sensitive requests, the API should first check this cache for active session metadata, fall back to the canonical sessions table on miss, and upsert the UNLOGGED cache row with a short TTL aligned to session invalidation requirements. Add indexes on token_hash and expires_at, and run periodic cleanup for expired rows to keep the table small. Because UNLOGGED tables are not crash-safe, treat this strictly as a rebuildable cache and repopulate from the durable sessions table after misses or database restart. Instrument hit rate, miss latency, and fallback query count so we can verify the cache removes session lookups from the hot path.

cells.context:
Relevant prior decisions for this task (from team memory):
- Hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations — Every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL; forward-only keeps prod and CI history linear
  - REJECTED: auto-generated migrations — A generated diff silently included a column drop that reached staging in 2026-03 — rolled back; generated DDL is no longer trusted for schema changes
  - REJECTED: down migrations — False confidence — most destructive changes cannot actually be reversed; restore-from-backup is the real rollback
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
  - REJECTED: Redis — A second datastore to provision, monitor, back up and secure — ops budget decision is one datastore until scale forces otherwise (revisit at 10x traffic)
  - REJECTED: in-process memory cache — Breaks under multiple API instances; sticky sessions rejected as deploy constraint
- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
  - REJECTED: Prisma — Query engine adds cold-start latency on serverless deploys and hides generated SQL; we hit both in the 2026-02 spike
  - REJECTED: TypeORM — Decorator-based entities fought our functional codebase style
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab

reply.proposal: I do not recommend enabling auto-generated migrations from the schema definition because the team explicitly rejected generated DDL after a destructive column drop reached staging. We should keep numbered, forward-only, hand-written SQL migrations as the only deployable migration artifact. To reduce the pain that motivated this request, I recommend adding CI validation that applies all migrations to a fresh Postgres database, verifies the resulting schema against the committed schema definition or schema snapshot, and fails on drift. We can also add a lightweight migration template/checklist so authors still write SQL manually but get consistent numbering, transaction guidance, and review prompts for destructive changes.

cells.context:
Relevant prior decisions for this task (from team memory):
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem
- API is versioned in the URL path (/v1), breaking changes bump the version — Explicit and cache-friendly; header versioning confused two integration partners in the spike
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
  - REJECTED: Redis — A second datastore to provision, monitor, back up and secure — ops budget decision is one datastore until scale forces otherwise (revisit at 10x traffic)
  - REJECTED: in-process memory cache — Breaks under multiple API instances; sticky sessions rejected as deploy constraint
- Feature flags read from environment variables, no flag service — A dozen flags do not justify a flag platform; env vars deploy atomically with the code

reply.proposal: Keep the public API RESTful under /v1 and add sparse fieldsets via query parameters, e.g. `GET /v1/users/123?fields[user]=id,displayName` or `GET /v1/orders?fields[order]=id,total,status`. Document allowed field names per resource in the OpenAPI spec, validate unknown or unauthorized fields with clear 400 responses, and ensure auth rules still apply before serialization. Implement field-aware serializers so omitted fields are not emitted, and where practical map requested fields down to SELECT projections to reduce database and serialization work. Include the fieldset query parameters in cache keys so HTTP caching remains correct and predictable for mobile clients.
acknowledged_rejections: GraphQL — previously rejected because resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface
tRPC — previously rejected because public consumers are not all TypeScript and it locks the contract to one language ecosystem
